content.explanation:
The fs (file system) module is one of Node.js's most important built-in modules, providing an API for interacting with the file system. Reading files is the most common file system operation, and Node.js offers three distinct APIs for it: the callback-based API (fs.readFile), the synchronous API (fs.readFileSync), and the promise-based API (fs/promises). Understanding when to use each is key to writing efficient Node.js code.

The callback-based fs.readFile(path, options, callback) is the original Node.js API. It reads the entire file asynchronously and calls the callback with an error (or null) and the file data when complete. If you do not specify an encoding (like 'utf8'), the callback receives a Buffer object containing raw binary data. With encoding specified, you get a string. The callback pattern follows the Node.js convention of error-first callbacks: the first argument is always the error, and the second is the result. This pattern predates Promises and is still widely used.

fs.readFileSync(path, options) is the synchronous version that blocks the event loop until the file is completely read. It returns the file content directly (no callback needed) and throws an error if something goes wrong. Use readFileSync only at application startup for reading configuration files or during build scripts where blocking is acceptable. Never use synchronous file operations in request handlers or hot paths of a server, as it blocks all other operations.

The modern and recommended approach is the promise-based API available through 'fs/promises' (or 'fs.promises'). It provides the same operations as the callback API but returns Promises, making it compatible with async/await. This is cleaner, easier to read, and easier to handle errors with try/catch. For example: 'const data = await fs.readFile("file.txt", "utf8")'. The fs/promises module was stabilized in Node.js 14 and is now the preferred way to work with the file system.

When reading files, understanding Buffers is important. A Buffer is Node.js's way of handling raw binary data. When you read a file without specifying encoding, you get a Buffer. You can convert it to a string with buffer.toString('utf8') or specify the encoding directly in the readFile options. Common encodings include 'utf8' (text files), 'ascii', 'base64' (for embedding binary data in strings), and 'hex'. For binary files like images or PDFs, work with Buffers directly rather than converting to strings.

codeExamples.title: Three Ways to Read a File
codeExamples.output:
Sync error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'example.txt'
Callback error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'example.txt'
Promise error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'example.txt'
codeExamples.explanation: All three approaches do the same thing but with different patterns. The sync version blocks execution. The callback version is non-blocking but uses nested callbacks. The promise version is non-blocking and supports clean async/await syntax. Always prefer fs/promises for new code.

codeExamples.title: Working with Buffers and Encodings
codeExamples.output:
Buffer: <Buffer 48 65 6c 6c 6f 2c 20 4e 6f 64 65 2e 6a 73 21>
Buffer hex: 48656c6c6f2c204e6f64652e6a7321
Buffer base64: SGVsbG8sIE5vZGUuanMh
Buffer utf8: Hello, Node.js!
Buffer length: 15 bytes

String length: 8
Buffer length: 10 (bytes, UTF-8 encoded)
codeExamples.explanation: Buffers represent raw binary data. Without encoding, readFile returns a Buffer. The emoji example shows that string length (character count) differs from buffer length (byte count) because multi-byte characters like emojis take 4 bytes in UTF-8.

codeExamples.title: Reading JSON and CSV Files
codeExamples.output:
JSON: { name: 'Node', version: 20 }
CSV: [ { name: 'Alice', age: '25' }, { name: 'Bob', age: '30' } ]
codeExamples.explanation: JSON files can be read as UTF-8 strings and parsed with JSON.parse(). For CSV files, split by newlines for rows and commas for columns. In production, use a library like csv-parse for handling quoted fields, escaping, and edge cases.

notes: Always use fs/promises with async/await for new code. The callback API is legacy and the sync API blocks the event loop.
The ENOENT error code means 'Error NO ENTry' — the file or directory does not exist. Always handle this error gracefully.
For large files, use streams (createReadStream) instead of readFile, which loads the entire file into memory at once.
commonPitfalls: Using readFileSync in server request handlers. This blocks the entire event loop and no other requests can be processed until the file is read.
Forgetting to specify 'utf8' encoding and getting a Buffer instead of a string. Always pass the encoding option when reading text files.
Not handling ENOENT errors. Always check if a file exists or use try/catch when reading files that might not be present.

exercises.title: Buffer to String Conversion
exercises.description: Create a Buffer from the string 'Node.js is awesome', then convert it to UTF-8, base64, and hex strings. Log each conversion on a separate line.
hints: Buffer.from(string) creates a buffer from a string
buffer.toString(encoding) converts the buffer to a string with the specified encoding

content.explanation:
Writing files is the counterpart to reading and is equally essential in Node.js applications. Common use cases include writing log files, generating reports, saving user data, creating configuration files, and caching computed results. Like reading, Node.js provides callback-based, synchronous, and promise-based APIs for writing files.

fs.writeFile(path, data, options, callback) writes data to a file, creating the file if it does not exist or completely replacing its contents if it does. The data can be a string, Buffer, or TypedArray. Options include encoding (default 'utf8'), mode (file permissions), and flag (default 'w' for write). The promise-based equivalent is fsPromises.writeFile(). Always use the promise-based API with async/await in new code. Be careful: writeFile overwrites the entire file by default. If you want to add content to the end of a file, use appendFile instead.

fs.appendFile(path, data, options, callback) adds data to the end of a file without erasing existing content. If the file does not exist, it creates it. This is commonly used for log files where you want to add entries over time. However, for high-frequency appending (like logging many events per second), appendFile opens and closes the file for each call, which is inefficient. In such cases, use a write stream or a logging library.

File flags control how the file is opened. The default flag for writeFile is 'w' (open for writing, create if needed, truncate if exists). Other useful flags include 'a' (append), 'wx' (write but fail if file exists, useful for preventing accidental overwrites), 'r+' (open for reading and writing without truncating), and 'ax' (append but fail if exists). Understanding flags gives you fine-grained control over file operations.

For writing large amounts of data or writing continuously over time, use fs.createWriteStream(). A write stream buffers data and writes it in chunks, which is much more memory-efficient than building a large string and calling writeFile once. Write streams emit events like 'finish' (all data flushed), 'error', and 'drain' (ready for more data after backpressure). The stream approach is essential for generating large files like CSV exports, log files, or data dumps without consuming excessive memory.

codeExamples.title: writeFile and appendFile
codeExamples.output:
File written
Lines appended
File contents:
Line 1
Line 2
Line 3

JSON file written
codeExamples.explanation: writeFile creates or overwrites the file. appendFile adds to the end without erasing. JSON.stringify with null and 2 as arguments produces pretty-printed JSON with 2-space indentation, making config files human-readable.

codeExamples.title: File Flags: Preventing Overwrites
codeExamples.output:
File created successfully
Second write blocked: EEXIST
Cleanup done
codeExamples.explanation: The 'wx' flag stands for 'write exclusive'. It creates a new file but throws EEXIST if the file already exists. This is useful for operations where accidentally overwriting an existing file would cause data loss.

codeExamples.title: Write Streams for Large Data
codeExamples.output:
All data written to file
File size: 3300 bytes
File deleted
codeExamples.explanation: createWriteStream is ideal for writing large amounts of data. It buffers writes internally and flushes them efficiently. The 'finish' event fires when all data has been flushed to the underlying file system. For very high throughput, monitor the 'drain' event to handle backpressure.

notes: writeFile completely replaces file contents. Use appendFile to add data to the end of an existing file.
When writing JSON, always use JSON.stringify(data, null, 2) for human-readable output. Omit the last two arguments for compact JSON in production data files.
For atomic writes (preventing partial file corruption on crash), write to a temporary file first, then rename it to the target path using fs.rename().
commonPitfalls: Using writeFile when you meant appendFile, accidentally erasing the file's existing contents.
Writing to files in server request handlers without proper error handling. File writes can fail due to permissions, disk space, or path issues.
Not using write streams for large data. Calling writeFile with a huge string consumes memory equal to the entire string size.

exercises.title: JSON Config Writer
exercises.description: Create a config object with properties: host ('localhost'), port (3000), and debug (true). Convert it to a pretty-printed JSON string using JSON.stringify with 2-space indentation, and log the result.
hints: JSON.stringify takes three arguments: the value, a replacer (null for all), and the indent level
Use 2 for the indent parameter to get readable formatting

content.explanation:
The path module is essential for working with file and directory paths in Node.js. It provides utilities for manipulating path strings in a cross-platform way, handling differences between Windows (backslashes) and POSIX systems like Linux and macOS (forward slashes). Never manually concatenate paths with string operations like 'dir + "/" + file' because this breaks on different operating systems and creates bugs with double slashes.

path.join() is the most commonly used method. It joins multiple path segments together using the platform-specific separator, normalizing the result. For example, path.join('/users', 'john', 'documents', 'file.txt') produces '/users/john/documents/file.txt' on Linux and '\\users\\john\\documents\\file.txt' on Windows. It also resolves '..' (parent directory) and '.' (current directory) segments. Always use path.join when combining path parts.

path.resolve() is similar to join but works differently: it resolves a sequence of paths into an absolute path, processing from right to left. If at any point an absolute path is encountered, the preceding segments are discarded. If no absolute path is encountered, the current working directory is prepended. For example, path.resolve('src', 'index.js') gives you an absolute path like '/home/user/project/src/index.js'. Use resolve when you need to guarantee an absolute path.

The path module provides several methods for extracting parts of a path. path.basename(filePath) returns the last portion (filename with extension). path.basename(filePath, ext) returns the filename without the specified extension. path.extname(filePath) returns the file extension including the dot (.js, .txt). path.dirname(filePath) returns the directory portion. path.parse(filePath) breaks a path into an object with root, dir, base, name, and ext properties. path.format(pathObject) does the reverse, building a path string from an object.

A critical concept is the difference between __dirname and process.cwd(). __dirname is always the directory of the current source file, regardless of where the Node process was started. process.cwd() returns the current working directory of the process, which depends on where the user ran the 'node' command from. When building paths to files relative to your source code (like templates, static assets, or config files), always use __dirname with path.join. When working with paths relative to the user's location (like file arguments), use process.cwd().

codeExamples.title: path.join vs path.resolve
codeExamples.output:
src/utils/helper.js
/users/jane/file.txt
a/c

resolve:
/home/user/project/src/index.js
/base/src/index.js
/override/file.js

join vs resolve:
join: /a/b
resolve: /b
codeExamples.explanation: path.join simply concatenates and normalizes. path.resolve works like navigating in a terminal: if it encounters an absolute path, it starts over from there. The difference between join('/a', '/b') -> '/a/b' and resolve('/a', '/b') -> '/b' is a common interview question.

codeExamples.title: Extracting Path Components
codeExamples.output:
basename: index.ts
basename (no ext): index
extname: .ts
dirname: /home/user/project/src

parsed: { root: '/', dir: '/home/user/project/src', base: 'index.ts', ext: '.ts', name: 'index' }
formatted: /home/user/project/dist/index.js

separator: /
delimiter: :
codeExamples.explanation: path.parse() returns all components of a path at once. path.format() reconstructs a path from components, which is useful when you need to change the extension or directory while keeping the filename.

codeExamples.title: __dirname vs process.cwd()
codeExamples.output:
__dirname: /home/user/project/src
cwd: /home/user/project
Template: /home/user/project/src/templates/email.html
Config: /home/user/project/config/default.json
User file: /home/user/project/user-input.txt

/home/user is absolute: true
src/index.js is absolute: false
codeExamples.explanation: Always use __dirname with path.join for paths relative to your source files. Use process.cwd() for paths relative to where the process was started. This distinction prevents bugs when your app is run from different directories.

notes: Never concatenate paths with + or template literals. Always use path.join() or path.resolve(). Manual concatenation breaks cross-platform compatibility.
path.resolve without arguments returns process.cwd(). path.resolve('file.txt') is equivalent to path.join(process.cwd(), 'file.txt').
In ES Modules, use import.meta.url with fileURLToPath and dirname to replicate __dirname: const __dirname = dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url));
commonPitfalls: Using string concatenation for paths: 'dir + "/" + file' breaks on Windows and creates double-slash issues. Always use path.join().
Confusing path.join and path.resolve. join simply concatenates; resolve produces absolute paths and resets on absolute segments.
Using process.cwd() when you meant __dirname. If the user runs your script from a different directory, cwd changes but __dirname stays the same.

exercises.title: Path Manipulation
exercises.description: Given the file path '/home/user/projects/app/src/index.ts', use the path module to extract and log: the filename with extension, the filename without extension, the file extension, and the parent directory. Each on a separate line.
hints: path.basename with one arg gives name + extension
path.basename with two args strips the given extension

exercises.title: Build a File Path
exercises.description: Use path.join to construct a path from __dirname, go up one directory ('..'), then into 'config', then 'database.json'. Also use path.resolve to turn the relative path 'src/utils/helpers.js' into an absolute path. Log both results.
hints: path.join concatenates segments with the correct separator
path.resolve creates an absolute path, prepending cwd if needed

content.explanation:
Beyond reading and writing file contents, you often need to work with the file system at a higher level: creating directories, listing directory contents, checking file metadata, renaming and deleting files, and watching for changes. The fs module provides all of these capabilities, and like file I/O, they come in callback, synchronous, and promise-based variants.

Creating directories is done with fs.mkdir(path, options). The most useful option is 'recursive: true', which creates all intermediate directories in the path if they do not exist (similar to 'mkdir -p' in shell). Without recursive, creating 'a/b/c' fails if 'a/b' does not exist. To remove a directory, use fs.rmdir (empty directories only) or fs.rm with the 'recursive: true' option (removes directory and all contents, like 'rm -rf'). The rm method with recursive was stabilized in Node.js 14.

fs.readdir(path, options) reads the contents of a directory and returns an array of filenames (strings). With the 'withFileTypes: true' option, it returns an array of Dirent objects that include type information (isFile(), isDirectory(), isSymbolicLink()). This is much more efficient than calling stat on each entry separately. For recursive directory listing, Node.js 18.17+ supports the 'recursive: true' option, which returns all files and subdirectories in the entire tree.

fs.stat(path) returns detailed metadata about a file or directory as a Stats object. Key properties include size (in bytes), birthtime (creation time), mtime (last modification time), atime (last access time), and methods like isFile(), isDirectory(), and isSymbolicLink(). The lstat variant does not follow symbolic links, returning info about the link itself rather than the target. Stat is useful for checking if a file exists, determining its size before reading, or implementing conditional logic based on modification time.

fs.rename(oldPath, newPath) moves or renames a file or directory. fs.unlink(path) deletes a file (not a directory). fs.copyFile(src, dest) copies a file. For watching file system changes, fs.watch(path, callback) monitors a file or directory for modifications. The callback receives the event type ('rename' or 'change') and the filename. fs.watch is useful for development tools, live reload, and file synchronization, though it has some platform-specific quirks. For production use, consider the chokidar library which provides a more reliable cross-platform API.

codeExamples.title: Creating and Reading Directories
codeExamples.output:
Directories created
demo/: [ 'README.md', 'src' ]
  FILE: README.md
  DIR: src

All files: [ 'README.md', 'src', 'src/index.js', 'src/utils', 'src/utils/helpers.js' ]
Cleaned up
codeExamples.explanation: mkdir with recursive:true creates all intermediate directories. readdir with withFileTypes:true returns Dirent objects so you can distinguish files from directories without extra stat calls. The recursive option in readdir lists the entire tree.

codeExamples.title: File Metadata with stat
codeExamples.output:
Is file: true
Is directory: false
Size: 13 bytes
Created: 2024-01-15T10:30:00.000Z
Modified: 2024-01-15T10:30:00.000Z
Accessed: 2024-01-15T10:30:00.000Z

File exists
File deleted
codeExamples.explanation: fs.stat provides detailed metadata including size, timestamps, and type information. Use fs.access to check if a file exists rather than the deprecated fs.exists. unlink deletes a single file.

codeExamples.title: Rename, Copy, and Watch Files
codeExamples.output:
File renamed
File copied
Files: [ 'backup.txt', 'renamed.txt' ]

fs.watch monitors files for changes in real-time
Events: "change" (content modified) or "rename" (file renamed/deleted)
Cleaned up
codeExamples.explanation: fs.rename moves or renames files and directories. fs.copyFile creates a duplicate. fs.watch observes file changes in real-time, useful for development tools. For production file watching, use the chokidar library for better cross-platform reliability.

notes: Always use { recursive: true } with mkdir when creating nested directories. Without it, the call fails if parent directories do not exist.
Use fs.access() instead of the deprecated fs.exists() to check if a file exists. fs.access checks both existence and permissions.
fs.rm with { recursive: true, force: true } is the equivalent of 'rm -rf'. Use with extreme caution and always validate paths before calling it.
commonPitfalls: Calling mkdir without recursive:true for nested paths. Each parent directory must exist for the call to succeed.
Using readdir without withFileTypes and then calling stat on each entry. withFileTypes is more efficient as it avoids extra system calls.

Trusting fs.watch completely. It has known cross-platform inconsistencies: it may fire duplicate events, miss events on some systems, or report different event types. Use chokidar for reliable watching.

exercises.title: Directory Structure Creator
exercises.description: Write an async function called 'scaffold' that uses fs/promises to create the following directory structure: 'project/src', 'project/tests', 'project/docs'. Use a single mkdir call with recursive:true for each. Then use readdir to list the contents of 'project' and log the result. Finally, clean up with fs.rm.
hints: mkdir with recursive:true creates parent directories automatically
readdir returns an array of entry names as strings

exercises.title: File Size Reporter
exercises.description: Write an async function that creates three files: 'a.txt' with content 'Hello' (5 bytes), 'b.txt' with content 'Hello World' (11 bytes), and 'c.txt' with content 'Hi' (2 bytes). Use fs.stat on each file to get its size, then log each filename and size in the format 'filename: X bytes'. Clean up all files afterward.
exercises.expectedOutput:
a.txt: 5 bytes
b.txt: 11 bytes
c.txt: 2 bytes
hints: fs.stat returns an object with a 'size' property in bytes
Use a for...of loop to await each stat call sequentially
